perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of New York applicable to contracts made and to be performed therein, without giving effect to its principles or rules regarding conflicts of laws, other than such principles directing application of New York law. The state and federal courts located in New York shall have non-exclusive jurisdiction and venue over any dispute arising out of or relating to this Agreement, and each Party consents to the personal jurisdiction and venue of these courts. Each Party waives any objection that it may now or hereafter have to the laying of venue of any such proceeding in any court in the state of New York and any claim that it may now or hereafter have that any such proceeding in any court in the state of New York has been brought in an inconvenient forum.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of New York applicable to contracts made and to be performed therein, without giving effect to its principles or rules regarding conflicts of laws, other than such principles directing application of New York law. Each Party consents to the personal jurisdiction and venue of these courts.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the clause where 'Each Party waives any objection that it may now or hereafter have to the laying of venue of any such proceeding in any court in the state of New York and any claim that it may now or hereafter have that any such proceeding in any court in the state of New York has been brought in an inconvenient forum,' the contract now allows either party to claim improper venue, even though the contract specifies New York law. This contradicts standard contract law principles regarding forum selection clauses.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Civil Practice Law and Rules (CPLR) § 501
perturbation.law_explanation: CPLR § 501 allows parties to agree on venue. The original clause reinforced this right. Removing the waiver of objection opens the door for a party to challenge the agreed-upon venue, potentially undermining the enforceability of the forum selection clause, contrary to the intent and provision of New York law allowing parties to pre-determine venue.

Contractual provisions fixing venue. Subject to the provisions of subdivision two of section five hundred ten and section five hundred fourteen of this article, written agreement fixing place of trial, made before an action is commenced, shall be enforced upon a motion for change of place of trial.
perturbation.justification: The original contract contained a clause waiving any objections to venue in New York courts. The changed text removes this waiver, potentially undermining the forum selection clause. Scraped Law Snippet 1 (CPLR § 501) states that 'written agreement fixing place of trial, made before an action is commenced, shall be enforced upon a motion for change of place of trial.' Removing the venue waiver allows a party to challenge the agreed-upon venue, creating a contradiction because the agreement to fix the venue could now be contested, weakening the enforceability that CPLR § 501 intends to provide.

perturbation.original_text: Each Party acknowledges that the other would be irreparably injured if the Receiving Party breaches any of its obligations under this Agreement. The Parties each agree that money damages would not be a sufficient remedy for any breach of this Agreement and that, in the event of a breach by a Party or its Representatives, the other Party shall be entitled to equitable relief, including injunction and specific performance, as a remedy for such breach. Such remedies shall not be deemed to be the exclusive remedies for a breach of this Agreement by a Party or its Representatives but shall be in addition to all other remedies available at law or equity to the non-breaching Party. Each Party expressly agrees to waive the defense that a remedy in damages will be adequate, and agrees to use its reasonable best efforts to cause its Representatives to waive, any requirement for the securing or posting of any bond in connection with any such remedy.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party acknowledges that the other would be irreparably injured if the Receiving Party breaches any of its obligations under this Agreement. The Parties each agree that money damages would not be a sufficient remedy for any breach of this Agreement and that, in the event of a breach by a Party or its Representatives, the other Party shall be entitled to equitable relief, including injunction and specific performance, as a remedy for such breach. Such remedies shall not be deemed to be the exclusive remedies for a breach of this Agreement by a Party or its Representatives but shall be in addition to all other remedies available at law or equity to the non-breaching Party.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'Each Party expressly agrees to waive the defense that a remedy in damages will be adequate, and agrees to use its reasonable best efforts to cause its Representatives to waive, any requirement for the securing or posting of any bond in connection with any such remedy,' the changed text allows a breaching party to argue that monetary damages are sufficient, even if the confidential information can't be retrieved, and also opens up the right to demand a bond for any injunction. This is a legal loophole that makes it harder to enforce specific performance/injunctive relief.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Law Regarding Injunctive Relief (CPLR Article 63)
perturbation.law_explanation: New York's CPLR Article 63 governs injunctions. While it doesn't explicitly mandate a bond, it allows the court to require one. The original clause waived this requirement by agreement. By removing this waiver, the modified contract allows a party to argue for a bond, potentially hindering or delaying injunctive relief, contrary to the purpose of ensuring immediate protection against breaches of confidentiality.

Grounds for preliminary injunction and temporary restraining order. A preliminary injunction may be granted in any action where it appears that the defendant threatens or is about to do, or is doing or procuring or suffering to be done, an
perturbation.justification: The original contract stipulates a waiver of the right to demand a bond in connection with equitable relief, thereby streamlining the process of obtaining an injunction. By removing this waiver, the modified text allows a party to argue for a bond, potentially hindering or delaying injunctive relief. According to the Law Explanation, this is contrary to the purpose of ensuring immediate protection against breaches of confidentiality. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing NY CPLR Article 63, indicates that a preliminary injunction may be granted, indirectly implying the possibility of requiring a bond as part of the process, which the original text sought to prevent. Thus, removing the waiver creates a loophole that goes against the original agreement, creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: In the event of litigation relating to this Agreement, if a court of competent jurisdiction determines that a Party or any of its Representatives have breached Page 4 of 5 this Agreement, then such Party shall be liable and pay to the other Party the reasonable legal fees and expenses incurred by the other Party in connection with such litigation, including any appeal therefrom.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event of litigation relating to this Agreement, if a court of competent jurisdiction determines that a Party or any of its Representatives have breached Page 4 of 5 this Agreement, then such Party may be liable and pay to the other Party the legal fees and expenses incurred by the other Party in connection with such litigation.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'shall be liable' to 'may be liable' and removing 'reasonable' and 'including any appeal therefrom,' the new contract introduces a loophole where the breaching party isn't automatically responsible for legal fees. It leaves the fee award to the court's discretion and excludes appeals, contradicting the intent of the original clause to fully indemnify the non-breaching party.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Contract Law Regarding Indemnification for Legal Fees
perturbation.law_citation: Chapin v. Chapin, 84 A.D.3d 1153, 924 N.Y.S.2d 470 (2d Dep't 2011)
perturbation.law_explanation: New York contract law generally enforces indemnification clauses for legal fees as agreed upon by the parties. While it doesn't mandate a specific wording, clauses that clearly indicate an intent to indemnify are usually upheld. By making the fee award discretionary and excluding appeals, the modified clause weakens the indemnification provision, creating uncertainty and potentially preventing the non-breaching party from fully recovering its legal costs, contrary to the principles of contract enforcement and indemnification.

perturbation.justification: The original text mandates that the breaching party 'shall be liable' for 'reasonable legal fees and expenses', including appeals. The modified text weakens this obligation by stating the breaching party 'may be liable' for 'legal fees and expenses,' removing the 'reasonable' qualifier and excluding appeals. According to the Perturbation Explanation, this creates a loophole where the breaching party isn't automatically responsible for legal fees and excludes appeals, contradicting the intent of the original clause. The Law Explanation further suggests this weakens the indemnification provision. Although there are no explicit snippets provided to analyze from official legal sources, the change from 'shall' to 'may' introduces a discretionary element that undermines the original intent for the breaching party to cover the legal fees.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party agrees that it will not use the Evaluation Material in any way directly or indirectly detrimental to the Disclosing Party. In particular, the Receiving Party agrees that it and its Representatives will not knowingly, as a result of knowledge or information obtained from the Evaluation Material or otherwise in connection with the Possible Transaction, directly or indirectly: (i) solicit, divert or attempt to solicit or divert any business or customer of the Disclosing Party or any of its affiliates; nor (ii) solicit, the employment of, employ, divert or attempt any of the foregoing with respect to, any employee of the Disclosing Party or any of its affiliates
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party agrees that it will not use the Evaluation Material in any way directly or indirectly detrimental to the Disclosing Party. In particular, the Receiving Party agrees that it and its Representatives will not solicit the employment of, employ, divert or attempt any of the foregoing with respect to, any employee of the Disclosing Party or any of its affiliates
perturbation.explanation: By removing the clause prohibiting the solicitation or diversion of business or customers, the modified text now permits the Receiving Party to actively poach clients from the Disclosing Party. This omission weakens the confidentiality protection and creates a legal loophole where the Receiving Party can use the confidential information to directly compete, going against typical NDA standards.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Trade Secrets Law (Generally, Unfair Competition)
perturbation.law_citation: Integrated Cash Management Services, Inc. v. Digital Transactions, Inc., 920 F.2d 171 (2d Cir. 1990)
perturbation.law_explanation: New York doesn't have a codified trade secrets act, but protects trade secrets under common law principles of unfair competition. Soliciting customers using confidential information gained during a business relationship can be considered unfair competition and a breach of fiduciary duty. By omitting the explicit prohibition against soliciting customers, the modified contract creates a gray area where such actions might be permissible, potentially conflicting with New York's protection against the misuse of confidential information to gain an unfair competitive advantage.

perturbation.justification: The original contract explicitly prohibits soliciting or diverting business/customers. The changed text omits this prohibition, allowing the Receiving Party to solicit the Disclosing Party's customers. The Law Explanation states that under New York common law principles, soliciting customers using confidential information gained during a business relationship can be considered unfair competition. The omission creates a legal loophole, potentially conflicting with New York's protection against the misuse of confidential information. Although the scraped snippets are not directly applicable as they do not specify the case of New York or specific laws about the solicitation of clients, the Law Explanation provides the necessary context that supports the contradiction.
